prompt_text: Best knowledge management alternatives for CS teams building self-service and guided onboarding experiences teams on a budget
response:
# Budget-Friendly Knowledge Management for CS Teams

## Free/Freemium Tier Options

**Documentation Platforms**
- **Notion** – Strong free tier; good for internal + customer-facing wikis
- **GitHub Pages + Jekyll** – Zero cost if you're already in GitHub ecosystem
- **Markdown + static site generators** (MkDocs, Docusaurus) – Self-hosted, minimal overhead

**Help Center Builders**
- **Slite** – Free version covers basic knowledge sharing
- **Slab** – Generous free tier for small teams
- **GitBook** – Free tier suitable for smaller orgs

## Low-Cost Paid Options

**Best Value**
- **HelpJuice** ($60-80/mo) – Purpose-built help centers, good search
- **Document360** ($99/mo) – Self-service portal focused, decent AI features
- **Crisp** ($25/mo base) – Lightweight; chat + KB combined

**If You Need Self-Service Analytics**
- **Helpscout Docs** ($50/mo) – Analytics showing what customers actually search for
- **Zendesk Guide** ($40+/mo) – Better for high-volume CS teams

## Strategic Approaches to Maximize ROI

**Hybrid Model**
- Internal docs in Notion (free)
- Customer-facing in HelpJuice (affordable)
- Chat support in native platform you likely have

**Content-First Strategy**
- Prioritize top 20% of customer questions first
- Use CS ticket data to identify gaps
- Iterative rather than perfect launch

**Automation to Reduce Costs**
- Set up automated ticket deflection (most platforms support this)
- Proactive help widgets on high-friction pages
- Chatbots for tier-1 questions (Intercom's free tier covers basics)

## Common Mistake to Avoid

Building comprehensive KB before validating customer usage patterns. Start with your 10 most common questions, then expand based on actual search/navigation data.

**What's your current stack?** That changes some recommendations (Intercom vs. native Zendesk vs. custom solution).
